<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562965]] Wikidata name: Unzen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Onsen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Unzen onsen jinja.JPG|250px]] | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]]小浜町雲仙319 | Coordinates = {{Coord|32|44|31.7|N|130|15|42.3|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Deity = 白日別命 外4柱 | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Type = Former Prefectural Shrine | Founding = Tradition: [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]]元年（[[:en:701|701]]） | Main Hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Ryuzō]] | Alternative Name = 四面宮 | Inscription, etc. = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Festival = October 29 | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=Onsen Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]] located in the hot spring town of Unzen in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]], 小浜町雲仙319.
Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ([[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.
== Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ([[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]), with Chikushi Province ([[:en:Tsukushi Province|Chikushi Province]]) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ([[:en:Hi Province|Higo Province]]) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ([[:en:Kumaso|Kumazokoku]]) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.
== History == === Origins === According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] ([[:en:701|701]]), [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.
=== From the Early Modern Period Onward === By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of [[:en:Ryūzōji Takanobu|Ryūzōji Takanobu]], [[:en:Arima Harunobu|Arima Harunobu]]—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ([[:en:1580|1580]], Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.
Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1869 ([[:en:1869|1869]]) during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1915 ([[:en:1915|1915]]) during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ([[:en:1916|1916]]) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.
Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Unzen Onsen|Unzen Onsen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562965]] Wikidata name: Unzen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Onsen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Unzen onsen jinja.JPG|250px]] | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]]小浜町雲仙319 | Coordinates = {{Coord|32|44|31.7|N|130|15|42.3|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Deity = 白日別命 外4柱 | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Type = Former Prefectural Shrine | Founding = Tradition: [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]]元年（[[:en:701|701]]） | Main Hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Ryuzō]] | Alternative Name = 四面宮 | Inscription, etc. = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Festival = October 29 | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=Onsen Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]] located in the hot spring town of Unzen in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]], 小浜町雲仙319.
Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ([[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.
== Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ([[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]), with Chikushi Province ([[:en:Tsukushi Province|Chikushi Province]]) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ([[:en:Hi Province|Higo Province]]) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ([[:en:Kumaso|Kumazokoku]]) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.
== History == === Origins === According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] ([[:en:701|701]]), [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.
=== From the Early Modern Period Onward === By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of [[:en:Ryūzōji Takanobu|Ryūzōji Takanobu]], [[:en:Arima Harunobu|Arima Harunobu]]—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ([[:en:1580|1580]], Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.
Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1869 ([[:en:1869|1869]]) during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1915 ([[:en:1915|1915]]) during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ([[:en:1916|1916]]) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.
Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Unzen Onsen|Unzen Onsen]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562965]] Wikidata name: Unzen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Onsen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Unzen onsen jinja.JPG|250px]] | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]]小浜町雲仙319 | Coordinates = {{Coord|32|44|31.7|N|130|15|42.3|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Deity = 白日別命 外4柱 | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Type = Former Prefectural Shrine | Founding = Tradition: [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]]元年（[[:en:701|701]]） | Main Hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Ryuzō]] | Alternative Name = 四面宮 | Inscription, etc. = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Festival = October 29 | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=Onsen Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]] located in the hot spring town of Unzen in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]], 小浜町雲仙319.
Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ([[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.
== Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ([[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]), with Chikushi Province ([[:en:Tsukushi Province|Chikushi Province]]) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ([[:en:Hi Province|Higo Province]]) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ([[:en:Kumaso|Kumazokoku]]) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.
== History == === Origins === According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] ([[:en:701|701]]), [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.
=== From the Early Modern Period Onward === By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of [[:en:Ryūzōji Takanobu|Ryūzōji Takanobu]], [[:en:Arima Harunobu|Arima Harunobu]]—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ([[:en:1580|1580]], Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.
Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1869 ([[:en:1869|1869]]) during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1915 ([[:en:1915|1915]]) during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ([[:en:1916|1916]]) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.
Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Unzen Onsen|Unzen Onsen]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562965]] Wikidata name: Unzen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Onsen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Unzen onsen jinja.JPG|250px]] | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]]小浜町雲仙319 | Coordinates = {{Coord|32|44|31.7|N|130|15|42.3|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Deity = 白日別命 外4柱 | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Type = Former Prefectural Shrine | Founding = Tradition: [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]]元年（[[:en:701|701]]） | Main Hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Ryuzō]] | Alternative Name = 四面宮 | Inscription, etc. = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Festival = October 29 | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=Onsen Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]] located in the hot spring town of Unzen in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]], 小浜町雲仙319.
Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ([[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.
== Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ([[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]), with Chikushi Province ([[:en:Tsukushi Province|Chikushi Province]]) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ([[:en:Hi Province|Higo Province]]) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ([[:en:Kumaso|Kumazokoku]]) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.
== History == === Origins === According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] ([[:en:701|701]]), [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.
=== From the Early Modern Period Onward === By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of [[:en:Ryūzōji Takanobu|Ryūzōji Takanobu]], [[:en:Arima Harunobu|Arima Harunobu]]—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ([[:en:1580|1580]], Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.
Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1869 ([[:en:1869|1869]]) during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1915 ([[:en:1915|1915]]) during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ([[:en:1916|1916]]) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.
Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Unzen Onsen|Unzen Onsen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562965]] Wikidata name: Unzen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Onsen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Unzen onsen jinja.JPG|250px]] | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]]小浜町雲仙319 | Coordinates = {{Coord|32|44|31.7|N|130|15|42.3|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Deity = 白日別命 外4柱 | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Type = Former Prefectural Shrine | Founding = Tradition: [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]]元年（[[:en:701|701]]） | Main Hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Ryuzō]] | Alternative Name = 四面宮 | Inscription, etc. = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Festival = October 29 | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=Onsen Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]] located in the hot spring town of Unzen in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]], 小浜町雲仙319.
Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ([[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.
== Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ([[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]), with Chikushi Province ([[:en:Tsukushi Province|Chikushi Province]]) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ([[:en:Hi Province|Higo Province]]) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ([[:en:Kumaso|Kumazokoku]]) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.
== History == === Origins === According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] ([[:en:701|701]]), [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.
=== From the Early Modern Period Onward === By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of [[:en:Ryūzōji Takanobu|Ryūzōji Takanobu]], [[:en:Arima Harunobu|Arima Harunobu]]—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ([[:en:1580|1580]], Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.
Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1869 ([[:en:1869|1869]]) during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1915 ([[:en:1915|1915]]) during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ([[:en:1916|1916]]) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.
Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Unzen Onsen|Unzen Onsen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Unzen-jinja]][[ja:温泉神社 (雲仙市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11562965]] Wikidata name: Unzen Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Onsen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Unzen onsen jinja.JPG|250px]] | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]]小浜町雲仙319 | Coordinates = {{Coord|32|44|31.7|N|130|15|42.3|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Deity = 白日別命 外4柱 | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Type = Former Prefectural Shrine | Founding = Tradition: [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]]元年（[[:en:701|701]]） | Main Hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Ryuzō]] | Alternative Name = 四面宮 | Inscription, etc. = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | Festival = October 29 | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=Onsen Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shrine]] located in the hot spring town of Unzen in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Unzen, Nagasaki|Unzen City]], 小浜町雲仙319.
Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ([[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]]), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.
== Worshipped deities (Saijin) == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ([[:en:Kyushu|Kyushu]]), with Chikushi Province ([[:en:Tsukushi Province|Chikushi Province]]) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ([[:en:Hi Province|Higo Province]]) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ([[:en:Kumaso|Kumazokoku]]) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.
== History == === Origins === According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] ([[:en:701|701]]), [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.
=== From the Early Modern Period Onward === By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of [[:en:Ryūzōji Takanobu|Ryūzōji Takanobu]], [[:en:Arima Harunobu|Arima Harunobu]]—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ([[:en:1580|1580]], Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.
Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1869 ([[:en:1869|1869]]) during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.
In 1915 ([[:en:1915|1915]]) during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ([[:en:1916|1916]]) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.
Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Unzen Onsen|Unzen Onsen]]

'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shrine}} located in the hot spring town of Unzen in {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}{{ill|Unzen, Nagasaki|en|Unzen, Nagasaki|WD=Q999453|lt=Unzen City}}, 小浜町雲仙319.

Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.

== Worshipped deities (Saijin) ==
{{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}}
The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ({{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}), with Chikushi Province ({{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi Province}}) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ({{ill|Hi Province|en|Hi Province|WD=Q1156510|lt=Higo Province}}) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ({{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|WD=Q1196897|lt=Kumazokoku}}) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.

== History ==
=== Origins ===
According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō}} ({{ill|701|en|701|WD=Q23496|lt=701}}), {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.

=== From the Early Modern Period Onward ===
By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shrine}} located in the hot spring town of Unzen in {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}{{ill|Unzen, Nagasaki|en|Unzen, Nagasaki|WD=Q999453|lt=Unzen City}}, 小浜町雲仙319.

Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.

== Worshipped deities (Saijin) ==
{{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}}
The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ({{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}), with Chikushi Province ({{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi Province}}) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ({{ill|Hi Province|en|Hi Province|WD=Q1156510|lt=Higo Province}}) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ({{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|WD=Q1196897|lt=Kumazokoku}}) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.

== History ==
=== Origins ===
According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō}} ({{ill|701|en|701|WD=Q23496|lt=701}}), {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.

=== From the Early Modern Period Onward ===
By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shrine}} located in the hot spring town of Unzen in {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}{{ill|Unzen, Nagasaki|en|Unzen, Nagasaki|WD=Q999453|lt=Unzen City}}, 小浜町雲仙319.

Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.

== Worshipped deities (Saijin) ==
{{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}}
The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ({{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu}}), with Chikushi Province ({{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi Province}}) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ({{ill|Hi Province|en|Hi Province|WD=Q1156510|lt=Higo Province}}) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ({{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|WD=Q1196897|lt=Kumazokoku}}) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.

== History ==
=== Origins ===
According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō}} ({{ill|701|en|701|WD=Q23496|lt=701}}), {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki}} initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.

=== From the Early Modern Period Onward ===
By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=Shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915|1=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916|1=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

| name = Onsen Shrine

| Main Hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Ryuzō|1=Nagare-zukuri}}

}}

'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the hot spring town of Unzen in {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}{{ill|Unzen, Nagasaki|en|Unzen, Nagasaki|WD=Q999453|lt=Unzen City|1=Unzen, Nagasaki}}, 小浜町雲仙319.

Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.

== Worshipped deities (Saijin) ==
{{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}}
The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ({{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu|1=Kyushu}}), with Chikushi Province ({{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ({{ill|Hi Province|en|Hi Province|WD=Q1156510|lt=Higo Province|1=Hi Province}}) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ({{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|WD=Q1196897|lt=Kumazokoku|1=Kumaso}}) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.

== History ==
=== Origins ===
According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|701|en|701|WD=Q23496|lt=701|1=701}}), {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.

=== From the Early Modern Period Onward ===
By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu|1=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu|1=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580|1=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=Shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915|1=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916|1=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

'''Onsen Shrine''' (温泉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the hot spring town of Unzen in {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}{{ill|Unzen, Nagasaki|en|Unzen, Nagasaki|WD=Q999453|lt=Unzen City|1=Unzen, Nagasaki}}, 小浜町雲仙319.

Managed as a {{ill|Bettōji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Bettō-ji|WD=Q11396755}} under the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ({{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}), the hot spring mountain {{ill|Manmyōji|ja|満明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmyōji|1=Manmyō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563890}} was established<ref name="unzen2019">{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author1=山﨑 功 |author2=和田 奈緒 |author3=Peemmaphat BUARAPHA |author4=出口智佳子 |title=雲仙・島原史の多文化研究 II―自然が育んだ雲仙山岳信仰の源流と現在・未来― |journal= |volume= |issue= |publisher=Saga University |date=2019 |pages=39-57 |url=https://saga-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/22439/files/yamazaki_201903.pdf}}</ref>. “Unzen” was originally written as “Onsen” (温泉), a nomenclature said to be derived from the mountain name of Manmyōji<ref>{{Cite journal |Language=Japanese |author=池永正人 |title=観光資源としての雲仙地獄 |journal=Onsen Regional Studies |issue=12 |date=2009 |pages=13-20 |url=https://onsenchiiki.jp/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/%E7%AC%AC12%E5%8F%B7.pdf}}</ref>.

== Worshipped deities (Saijin) ==
{{see also|{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}}
The deities enshrined are Shirahiwake no Mikoto (白日別命), Hayahiwake no Mikoto (速日別命), Toyohiwake no Mikoto (豊日別命), Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake no Mikoto (建日向日豊久士比泥別命), and Takehiwake no Mikoto (建日別命). In the upper volume of the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, it is stated that a deity with four faces in one body protects Kyushu ({{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|WD=Q13987|lt=Kyushu|1=Kyushu}}), with Chikushi Province ({{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}) associated with Shirahiwake, Toyokuni with Toyohiwake, Higo Province ({{ill|Hi Province|en|Hi Province|WD=Q1156510|lt=Higo Province|1=Hi Province}}) with Takehimukahito Yokuji Hinewake, and Kumagusu ({{ill|Kumaso|en|Kumaso|WD=Q1196897|lt=Kumazokoku|1=Kumaso}}) with Takehiwake; hence, it is also known as “Four-Faced Shrine”.

== History ==
=== Origins ===
According to the "Onsenzan Engi-sho" (温泉山縁起書), in the first year of {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} ({{ill|701|en|701|WD=Q23496|lt=701|1=701}}), {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} initiated a great vow to propagate Buddhist teachings and petitioned Emperor Monmu for the construction of a temple complex. In response, land was donated, leading to the establishment of Manmyōji and the enshrinement of the Four-Faced Bodhisattva (Onsen Shrine) as the guardian of the hot spring mountain<ref name="unzenvc">{{Cite web |Language=Japanese |title=温泉山の繁栄と衰退 |url=https://unzenvc.com/_userdata/%E9%9B%B2%E4%BB%99%E6%AD%B4%E5%8F%B212345.pdf |publisher=Unzen Visitor Center, Unzen Mountain Information Center |accessdate=2024-08-12}}</ref>. The "Chronicle of Guardian Deities through the Ages" also records that in the first year of Taihō, in the Xin Chou year, traces of the Onsen Shrine in Hishū Takara District were noted, marking its enshrinement in 701 CE<ref name="unzenvc" />.

=== From the Early Modern Period Onward ===
By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu|1=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu|1=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580|1=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=Shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915|1=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916|1=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.

=== From the Early Modern Period Onward ===
By the latter half of the 16th century, conflicts arose between Christian missionaries in Kyushu and yamabushi (practitioners of Shugendō)<ref name="unzenvc" />. Under the threat of {{ill|Ryūzōji Takanobu|en|Ryūzōji Takanobu|WD=Q1076150|lt=Ryūzōji Takanobu|1=Ryūzōji Takanobu}}, {{ill|Arima Harunobu|en|Arima Harunobu|WD=Q1197394|lt=Arima Harunobu|1=Arima Harunobu}}—with political considerations—received Christian baptism in 1580 ({{ill|1580|en|1580|WD=Q6712|lt=1580|1=1580}}, Tenshō 8), after which temples and shrines in his domain were suppressed<ref name="unzen2019" /><ref name="unzenvc" />. Furthermore, during the Shimabara Rebellion (Shimabara-Tenshō Ikkō), Onsenzan Manmyōji lost all its buildings<ref name="unzenvc" />.

Subsequently, under the lordship of Takaraki Tadayoshi, Manmyōji and other structures were rebuilt, reviving the syncretic mountain worship of Shinto and Buddhism<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1869 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}}) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} period, it was renamed Chikushi Kunidama Shrine, and further, in 1872 during the Meiji Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree ({{ill|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Separation of Shinto and Buddhism Decree|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=Shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}), practitioners such as shugendō monks returned to secular life, with Onsen Shrine and Manmyōji Ichijōin becoming independent<ref name="unzen2019" />.

In 1915 ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915|1=1915}}) during the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} period, it reverted to the name Onsen Shrine, and in 1916 ({{ill|1916|en|1916|WD=Q2087|lt=1916|1=1916}}) it was elevated to the status of a prefectural shrine.

Near the summit of Mount Unzen, the Oku-no-miya, Fugen Shrine, once existed but was damaged by an eruption (the eruption is said to have occurred in the vicinity of this shrine) and was later rebuilt.
